text: Hello everyone, welcome to another video presentation on laparoscopic colorectal surgery. Today we are going to show you our technique of complete laparoscopic sigmoid and rectal mobilization in a male patient.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the aorta and extends the incision cranially towards the anticipated origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) approximately three centimeters above its origin, taking care to avoid the surrounding sympathetic plexus.

The surgeon identifies and divides the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) located lateral to the previously divided inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection plane is positioned just posterior to these divided vessels, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels beneath the medial to lateral mesentery dissection plane.
The surgeon advances to the posterior dissection plane of the mesorectum, preparing to mobilize the sigmoid and rectum laparoscopically.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the posterior avascular plane, avoiding proximity to the aortic bifurcation and sacral promontory to protect the superior hypogastric plexus. The dissection progresses caudally, with caution exercised to avoid using monopolar diathermy near pelvic veins to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon and left colon from their lateral pelvic attachments, achieving complete mobilization of the lower left colon.
The surgeon uses an endostapler to divide the sigmoid colon, facilitating the pelvic dissection. The divided colon is visible, but this step is not routinely performed to minimize operational costs.
The surgeon incises the peritoneal fold anterior to the recto-vesical pouch, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues with the lateral dissection deep into the pelvis, carefully avoiding excessive lateral dissection to preserve the right and left hypogastric nerves and the inferior hypogastric plexus.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection, revealing the seminal vesicles. A thin, consistent plane of dissection is visible through the Denonvilliers fascia, extending up to the base of the prostate.
The surgeon mobilizes the lowermost rectum by dividing the fascial attachments to the lateral and posterior pelvic wall, exposing the pelvic floor.
The surgeon separates the rectum from the prostate, revealing the distinct anatomical boundary between these two structures.

After completing the dissection, the muscular and rectal junction is visible, devoid of fat. The specimen is delivered perineally, revealing the glistening bilobed appearance of the mesorectum posterior to the rectum.
